retrieved_docs.title: Ewing Sarcoma -- Surgical Oncology -- Metastatic Disease
retrieved_docs.content: While the metastatic disease has a poor prognosis, patients with isolated pulmonary metastasis may be amenable to resection. Metastatectomy in selected patients had a 3-year overall survival of 60% and event-free survival of 45%. [19] Candidates should be selected on a case-by-case basis.

retrieved_docs.title: Ewing Sarcoma -- Medical Oncology -- Recurrent Disease
retrieved_docs.content: The rEECur trial is a multiarm Phase II/III trial that investigated the use of several agents, including topotecan and cyclophosphamide, irinotecan and temozolomide, gemcitabine and docetaxel, or high-dose ifosfamide. This trial demonstrated the superiority of high-dose ifosfamide in prolonging survival, with a median survival of 16.8 months. Other systemic agents, such as eribulin in combination with irinotecan and nab-paclitaxel with gemcitabine, are underway (NCT03245450) (NCT02945800). Targeted molecular agents have also been of interest. Cabozantinib, a tyrosine kinase inhibitor, has demonstrated antitumor activity in heavily pre-treated patients. [60] Ganitumab, an anti-IGFR antibody, combined with palbociclib, a CDK4 inhibitor, produced disappointing results (NCT04129151). [3] Other agents targeting the EWS-FL11 fusion protein, such as TK216, are under investigation (NCT02657005). [3] Immunotherapy agents targeting PDL-1 with pembrolizumab or nivolumab have not produced tumor responses. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Ewing Sarcoma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Combining immunohistochemical and morphological findings with relevant clinical history can help narrow down the differential diagnosis of Ewing sarcoma that includes other small round cell tumors such as neuroblastoma, rhabdomyosarcoma, lymphoma, neuroectodermal tumors, desmoplastic small round cell tumor, and synovial sarcoma. Also, consider osteomyelitis, osteogenic sarcoma, and eosinophilic granuloma in the differential diagnosis of Ewing sarcoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Ewing Sarcoma -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Over the last 40 years, both local therapy and multiagent adjuvant chemotherapy have achieved considerable progress in the treatment of localized disease that improved the 5-year survival rate from less than 20% to greater than 70%, but the recurrence rate remains high. Approximately 25% of patients with initially localized disease ultimately relapse. No standard therapy exists for relapsed and refractory Ewing sarcoma, with survival rates being less than 30% in those with isolated lung metastases and less than 20% in those with bone and bone marrow involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: Ewing Sarcoma -- Surgical Oncology -- Amputation
retrieved_docs.content: Amputations are infrequently performed but might be considered in cases where achieving a negative margin is not feasible with less radical approaches, in limbs with poor baseline function, or instances of locally recurrent disease. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Ewing Sarcoma -- Medical Oncology -- Recurrent Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who relapse after definitive treatment have poor outcomes, with a 5-year survival of 13%. [57] The time to recurrence is considered a predictor for post-recurrence survival, with patients >/= 2 years having a 5-year survival of 30% versus those <2 years at 7%. [57] While most relapses occur within the first 2 years of treatment, long-term relapses can occur. [58] The cumulative risk of recurrence at 20 years is 13%. [59]

retrieved_docs.title: Ewing Sarcoma -- Medical Oncology -- Recurrent Disease
retrieved_docs.content: The lack of response may result from low PDL expression (19% of all tumor cell samples), limited tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes, and a low tumor mutational burden. [3] High-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell transplantation (HDT-ASCT) and no current prospective clinical trials are in progress. [3] This is an intensive systemic regimen that only a select number of candidates with relapse may be eligible to receive. Single-institution experience using HDT-ASCT demonstrated a 10-year overall survival of 46%, including relapsed and metastatic Ewing sarcoma. Patients with early relapse <2 years have an especially poor OS. [61]

retrieved_docs.title: Rhabdomyosarcoma -- Staging -- Low-risk subset 2
retrieved_docs.content: Stage 1, CG III, nonorbital site, fusion-negative Stage 3, CG I or II, fusion negative

retrieved_docs.title: Rhabdomyosarcoma -- Staging -- High-risk
retrieved_docs.content: Stage IV, CG IV, any fusion status

retrieved_docs.title: ALK Negative Anaplastic Large Cell Lymphoma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Biopsies of ALK(-) ALCL are challenging and require exclusion of other malignancies. ALK(-) ALCL can mimic other non-hematologic malignancies such as sarcomas, carcinomas, germ cell tumors, and melanomas both cytologically and histologically. This is especially true in lesions characterized predominately by anaplasia and pleomorphism when “hallmark cells” with horseshoe-shaped or wreath-like nuclei are difficult to appreciate [20] . Besides nuclear morphology, the architectural pattern of ALCL can mimic other non-hematologic malignancies as both ALK(-) ALCL, and metastatic carcinoma tend to invade lymph node sinuses. Special care should be taken in small specimens, especially the fine-needle aspiration (FNA) specimen.  There have been several case reports describing cytomorphologic features of ALCL mimicking soft tissue sarcomas on FNA biopsy specimens [23] [24] .

retrieved_docs.title: Ewing Sarcoma -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Distal extremity involvement in Ewing sarcoma is associated with a more favorable prognosis, than patients with lesions in proximal extremities. [64] Metastatic disease presence crucially impacts prognosis assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: Leiomyosarcoma -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Entrectinib has approval for patients who have a solid tumor that harbors the NTRK gene fusion without a known acquired resistance mutation, are metastatic, or where surgical resection is likely to result in severe morbidity, and have progressed following treatment or have no satisfactory alternative therapy. The FDA approved Entrectinib in April 2019 based on an integrated analysis of phase 2 STARTRK-2, phase 1 STARTRK-1, and the phase 1 ALKA-372-001 trials. These trials were conducted across 15 countries and 150 clinical trial sites. Entrectinib demonstrated an objective response rate of 57%.

retrieved_docs.title: Rhabdomyosarcoma -- Staging -- Intermediate-risk
retrieved_docs.content: Stage 2 or 3, CG III, fusion negative Stage 4, CG IV, patient age <10 years, fusion-negative Stage 1 to 3, CG I to III, fusion-positive

retrieved_docs.content: This study led to VDC/IE being the standard of care in the United States. The chemotherapy is started before the local therapy and then continued postoperatively if there is no evidence of progression.

retrieved_docs.title: Rhabdomyosarcoma -- Staging -- Low-risk subset 1
retrieved_docs.content: Stage 1 or 2, CG I to III, fusion-negative Stage 1, CG III, orbital site only, fusion-negative

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1898
retrieved_docs.content: The CLINICAL CLASSIFICATION OF NEOPLASMS OF THE MUSCULOSKELETAL SYSTEM AND SOFT TISSUES encompasses specific subcategories for metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head and for metastatic malignant neoplasm to the supraclavicular region. By defining these child categories, the classification aids clinicians in recognizing patterns of metastasis from musculoskeletal and soft‑tissue tumors to distinct anatomical sites, which is essential for accurate staging, targeted diagnostic work‑up, and formulation of appropriate therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2192
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face is a specific type of neoplasm of bone of the face, representing a malignant tumor that originates in the facial bones. This relationship indicates that the broader category “neoplasm of bone of the face” encompasses the more specific entity “primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face,” clarifying the hierarchical classification of facial bone tumors for diagnostic and therapeutic considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1119
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to frontal bone is classified as a malignant neoplasm of frontal bone, and Metastatic malignant neoplasm to parietal bone is classified as a malignant neoplasm of parietal bone. Both of these site‑specific malignant neoplasms are encompassed by the broader diagnostic category cancer of bone and connective tissue, which is also referred to in Arabic as ورم العظام. By mapping the malignant neoplasms of the frontal and parietal bones to cancer of bone and connective tissue, clinicians can recognize these metastatic lesions as part of the overall spectrum of bone‑related cancers, facilitating accurate classification, staging, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2187
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is a malignant condition that involves the skeletal system and its supporting connective tissues. It has a broader relationship with ورم العظام, indicating that bone tumors (ورم العظام) are encompassed within this wider category of cancer. Recognizing that ورم العظام represents a specific manifestation of cancer of bone and connective tissue aids clinicians in accurate diagnosis, appropriate classification, and targeted therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1192
retrieved_docs.content: The CLINICAL CLASSIFICATION OF NEOPLASMS OF THE MUSCULOSKELETAL SYSTEM AND SOFT TISSUES encompasses specific subcategories for metastatic malignant neoplasms, including Metastatic malignant neoplasm to head, Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular region, and the Arabic‑language category نقائل إلى الرقبة (metastatic neoplasm to the neck). These child categories delineate common distant sites to which musculoskeletal and soft‑tissue malignancies may spread, aiding clinicians in organizing disease staging, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and planning targeted therapeutic strategies for patients with metastatic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of the disease سرطانة العظم. In patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of metastatic lesions indicates that malignant cells have spread to bone from another primary site, while secondary lesions represent additional tumor deposits that develop as a consequence of the primary bone malignancy. Recognizing these lesions is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of appropriate treatment strategies for سرطانة العظم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are the abnormal cells that define the disease known as سرطانة العظم (bone cancer).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2894
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding that indicates the presence of سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). The detection of malignant cellular infiltrate in tissue samples signals that cancerous cells have invaded the bone, serving as an important diagnostic marker for confirming bone malignancy and guiding subsequent therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: cancer; other and unspecified primary and سرطان الغدة الدرقية are both classified as subtypes of ورم صماوي خبيث, which denotes a malignant tumor. This relationship indicates that these specific cancers fall under the broader category of malignant tumors, sharing the characteristic of uncontrolled, invasive cell growth. Recognizing them as forms of ورم صماوي خبيث helps guide diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 246
retrieved_docs.content: these relationships integrate organ‑specific primary malignancies, metastatic pathways to lymph nodes and bone, and broader cancer classification schemes, providing a structured framework essential for accurate diagnosis, coding, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2190
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the parietal bone is classified as a malignant neoplasm of the parietal bone, indicating that cancer that has spread to this skull bone falls within the broader category of primary malignant bone tumors of the parietal region. Recognizing this relationship aids clinicians in accurately diagnosing, staging, and formulating appropriate therapeutic strategies for patients with metastatic involvement of the parietal bone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to ethmoid bone, metastatic malignant neoplasm to frontal bone, and metastatic malignant neoplasm to temporal bone (diagnosis) are each classified as malignant neoplasms of the ethmoid bone, frontal bone, and temporal bone respectively. These site‑specific malignant bone tumors are encompassed within the broader diagnostic category “cancer of bone and connective tissue,” which is mapped to each of the three bone‑specific malignant neoplasms. Consequently, the three metastatic bone cancers are recognized as particular manifestations of bone and connective tissue cancer, highlighting their shared pathology while preserving their distinct anatomical locations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2902
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are abnormal cells that characterize cancers classified as other and unspecified primary, indicating that these atypical cells constitute the pathological basis of such unspecified primary malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2188
retrieved_docs.content: The metastatic malignant neoplasm to the frontal bone is classified as a malignant neoplasm of the frontal bone, indicating that a cancerous tumor that has spread to the frontal bone falls within the broader category of primary malignant tumors arising in that bone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1151
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to ethmoid bone, metastatic malignant neoplasm to occipital bone, and metastatic malignant neoplasm to temporal bone (diagnosis) are each classified as malignant neoplasm of ethmoid bone, malignant neoplasm of occipital bone, and malignant neoplasm of temporal bone respectively. These site‑specific malignant bone tumors are encompassed within the broader diagnostic category of cancer of bone and connective tissue, to which each of the three bone‑specific neoplasms is mapped. Recognizing that metastatic lesions to the ethmoid, occipital, and temporal bones represent distinct manifestations of bone and connective tissue cancer aids clinicians in accurate coding, staging, and treatment planning for patients with skeletal metastases.
